perturbation.original_text: Company warrants that the Products will be designed, manufactured, constructed, assembled and packaged in a workmanlike manner and that such Products shall be fully functional and fit for their intended purposes. Company further warrants that the Products sold hereunder shall be free from defects in design, materials and workmanship for a period of twenty-four (24) months after delivery to Distributor's end-user. The Company shall not be liable for defective Products, except as provided in this Agreement. The Distributor at all times shall comply with all requirements of the Magnuson-Moss Warranty-Federal Trade Commission Improvement Act and similar federal and state laws and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Company warrants that the Products will be designed, manufactured, constructed, assembled and packaged in a workmanlike manner and that such Products shall be fully functional and fit for their intended purposes. Company further warrants that the Products sold hereunder shall be free from defects in design, materials and workmanship for a period of twenty-four (24) months after delivery to Distributor's end-user. The Company shall not be liable for defective Products, except as provided in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the sentence stating 'The Distributor at all times shall comply with all requirements of the Magnuson-Moss Warranty-Federal Trade Commission Improvement Act and similar federal and state laws and regulations' from the warranty section, the contract becomes less compliant. This is because this act makes consumer product warranties more readily understandable and enforceable and placing the compliance language within the warranty section is essential.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act requires that warranties be written in clear and readily understood language, and that they be available for consumers to read before buying. Removing the requirement for the distributor to comply with it from the warranty section undermines the act's goal of ensuring clear and conspicuous warranty information.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 50 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC Ch. 50 : CONSUMER PRODUCT WARRANTIES From Title 15—COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 50 —CONSUMER PRODUCT WARRANTIES Sec. 2301. Definitions. 2302. Rules governing contents of warranties. 2303. Designation of written warranties. 2304. Federal minimum standards for warranties. 2305. Full and limited warranting of a consumer product. 2306. Service contracts; rules for full, clear and conspicuous disclosure of terms and conditions; addition to or in lieu of written warranty. 2307. Designation of representatives by warrantor to perform duties under written or implied warranty. 2308. Implied warranties. 2309. Procedures applicable to promulgation of rules by Commission. 2310. Remedies in consumer disputes. 2311. Applicability to other laws. 2312. Effective dates. §2301. Definitions For the purposes of this chapter: (1) The term "consumer product" means any tangible personal property which is distributed in commerce and which is normally used for personal, family, or household purposes (including any such property intended to be attached to or installed in any real property without regard to whether it is so attached or installed). (2) The term "Commission" means the Federal Trade Commission. (3) The term "consumer" means a buyer (other than for purposes of resale) of any consumer product, any person to whom such product is transferred during the duration of an implied or written warranty (or service contract) applicable to the product, and any other person who is entitled by the terms of such warranty (or service contract) or under applicable State law to enforce against the warrantor (or service contractor) the obligations of the warranty (or service contract). (4) The term "supplier" means any person engaged in the business of making a consumer product directly or indirectly available to consumers. (5) The term "warrantor" means any supplier or other person who gives or offers to give a written warranty or who is or may be obligated under an implied warranty. (6) The term "written warranty" means— (A) any written affirmation of fact or written promise made in connection with the sale of a consumer product by a s
perturbation.justification: The original text included a clause requiring the distributor to comply with the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act, which ensures warranties are clear and understandable. The changed text removes this compliance requirement. The Law Explanation states that removing this requirement undermines the act's goal of ensuring clear warranty information. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references 15 USC Ch. 50, which is the Consumer Product Warranties chapter, and mentions sections like 'Rules governing contents of warranties' and 'Remedies in consumer disputes,' highlighting the importance of warranty compliance. Removing the clause directly contradicts the purpose of the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act, creating a legal contradiction because the distributor is no longer explicitly obligated to adhere to it within the context of the warranty.

perturbation.original_text: Time is of the essence of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Time is of the essence of this Agreement. Company may choose to adjust the prices for Products in the first Product Year, and as such, will be supplied by Company as Exhibit C.
perturbation.explanation: Moving pricing from its original section (2.4) and placing a statement about it in the 'Time is of the Essence' section creates confusion about pricing terms. Pricing is a key element of the contract, and its explicit terms must be clearly defined and consistent; otherwise, it may introduce unenforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-305. Open Price Term.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-305 governs open price terms in contracts for the sale of goods. By stating that time is of the essence, yet vaguely referring to Exhibit C for initial pricing with a statement about adjusting pricing, the contract fails to establish clear and definite pricing terms. This contravenes the UCC's requirements for clarity and certainty in commercial agreements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-305. Open Price Term. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-305. Open Price Term. (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) nothing is said as to price; or (b) the price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith . (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as cancelled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract . In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a structural flaw by placing pricing information within a section dedicated to 'Time is of the Essence,' which is irrelevant to pricing. This violates the clarity required for contract terms, especially regarding pricing, potentially leading to unenforceability. The Law Explanation points to UCC § 2-305 regarding open price terms, emphasizing the need for clear pricing in commercial agreements. While Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC § 2-305, allows for contracts to be concluded even if the price is not settled, it also specifies conditions where a 'reasonable price' must be determined or agreed upon. By obscuring the pricing terms, the changed text makes it difficult to ascertain a clear price, as is legally required, thus creating a legal contradiction, as defined under 'Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction.'

perturbation.original_text: Any notice or other communication required or permitted hereunder shall be in writing and shall be delivered personally, telegraphed, telexed, or sent by facsimile transmission or sent by certified, registered or express mail, postage prepaid. Any such notice shall be deemed given when so delivered personally, Page -12- telegraphed, telexed or sent by facsimile transmission or, if mailed, two (2) business days after the date of deposit in the United States mail, by certified mail return receipt requested, as follows: If to the Distributor to: Electric City of Illinois L.L.C. 8628 Oketo Avenue Bridgeview, IL 60455 Facsimile No. (708) 598-4671 Attn: Jim Stumpe With a copy to: Thomas V. McCauley 200 W. Adams, Suite 2500 Chicago, IL 60606 Facsimile No. (312) 346-9316 If to Company to: Electric City Corp. 1280 Lanmeier Rd. perturbation.changed_text: Any notice or other communication required or permitted hereunder shall be in writing and shall be delivered personally, telegraphed, telexed, or sent by facsimile transmission or sent by certified, registered or express mail, postage prepaid. Any such notice shall be deemed given when so delivered personally, Page -12- telegraphed, telexed or sent by facsimile transmission or, if mailed, two (2) business days after the date of deposit in the United States mail. Facsimile No. (708) 598-4671 Attn: Jim Stumpe With a copy to: Thomas V. McCauley 200 W. Adams, Suite 2500 Chicago, IL 60606 Facsimile No. (312) 346-9316 If to Company to: Electric City Corp. 1280 Lanmeier Rd. Elk Grove Village, IL 60007 Attn: Joseph Marino, President With a copy to: Kwaitt & Ruben, Ltd. 211 Waukegan Road Suite 300 Northfield, Illinois 60093 Attn: Philip E. Ruben, Esq.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'by certified mail return receipt requested, as follows: If to the Distributor to: Electric City of Illinois L.L.C. 8628 Oketo Avenue Bridgeview, IL 60455' the company can now say they did not receive a formal notification for any potential issue. This can create severe problems for distributors trying to follow up, especially as it removes the Illinois address to send information. Having a physical address as well as contact name is essential for formal correspondence.
perturbation.law_explanation: 810 ILCS 5/1-309 states a person has notice of a fact if the person has reason to know it exists from all the facts and circumstances known to the person at the time in question. Removing the specific address information reduces the enforceability to where the notice or information should be sent, causing potential legal issues.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for certified mail with return receipt requested and the distributor's address, which weakens the enforceability of notice provisions. The Law Explanation states that removing the specific address information causes potential legal issues, in alignment with the 'Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction' definition because a required element (address) was removed, creating a contradiction because the distributor now is unable to show they sent formal notification, which would be hard to prove without return receipt requested. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to Illinois Compiled Statutes (ILCS), highlighting the importance of accurate legal references and adherence to statutory requirements, something that the altered contract goes against.
